MESSAGE: “Get-LocalUser | ForEach-Object { $userName = $_.Name $output = net user $userName $lastLogonLine = $output | Where-Object { $_ -match '^ *Last logon' } if ($lastLogonLine) { if ($lastLogonLine -match 'Last logon\s+(.*)') { $lastLogon = $matches[1].Trim() } else { $lastLogon = \"Not found\" } } else { $lastLogon = \"Not found\" } [PSCustomObject]@{ UserName = $userName LastLogon = $lastLogon } } | ConvertTo-Json -Compress " ParentUser: TCT\tctrtm.”
SYMPTOM: WithSecure or Adlumin detects the NET USER commands but does not identify CS as the cause.
INFO: EDR platforms including Adlumin and WithSecure attribute activity based on the immediate process such as powershell.exe or net.exe and do not always retain full parent process lineage. As a result EDR telemetry may not explicitly identify ConnectSecureAgent as the initiator even though the activity originates from a ConnectSecure scan.

MESSAGE: INT API failed with status code 429
SYMPTOM: Partner will not be able to scan fortigate firewall due to rate limit exceeded.
INFO: FortiGate REST API enforces built-in rate limits and will return an HTTP 429 status code when these are exceeded. By default, FortiGate allows approximately: 100 GET/monitor API calls per second; 30 configuration write calls per second. If these thresholds are crossed, the firewall will block additional requests from the same source IP using a sliding time window. To resolve this: Please ensure that requests from the Agent/Probe system are prioritized and not restricted by the firewall. Consider applying rate limiting or distributing API calls over time to prevent hitting the FortiGate rate limit. Create a new profile with new REST API key and map it in the same company where the issue is occurring, and remove the old credential from the ConnectSecure Portal. After mapping the new credentials, initiate a fresh firewall scan.
REF: Please find the KB link to create new REST API key.

MESSAGE: Socket connection closed.
SYMPTOM: Firewall job will fail on Ubiquiti EdgeRouter
INFO: This issue is commonly caused by device-side session policies or management restrictions. We recommend reviewing the following configurations on the Ubiquiti EdgeRouter: 1. SSH / CLI Idle Timeout: Check whether the idle timeout is set too low (for example, 60 seconds) and increase it if needed to prevent premature session closure. 2. Concurrent Session Limits: Ensure the device is not terminating active SSH sessions due to maximum session limits or session recycling rules. 3. System Logs: Review system logs for dropped-session entries to identify why the SSH connection is being closed. 4. Management Interface Policies: Confirm that the management interface is not enforcing restrictive policies that may terminate sessions early, block automated scanning tools, or reset SSH connections.

MESSAGE: ssh: handshake failed: EOF
SYMPTOM: ESXI hosts creds not validating
INFO: An SSH connection was initiated, but it was closed before the handshake could complete. This indicates that while the TCP connection may be established, the remote side is closing it unexpectedly, resulting in an EOF (End-Of-File) condition. To help identify the root cause, please run the following checks from the affected device: 1. Check whether the SSH banner is received using: nc 172.25.254.26 22. You should see a response such as SSH-2.0-OpenSSH. If no banner appears, the SSH service may not be running properly. 2. Run SSH in verbose mode using: ssh -vvv <user>@172.25.254.26. Please share the output to help determine at which stage the handshake is failing.

MESSAGE: No successful SNMP responses received with any community string
SYMPTOM: Snmp creds not validating
INFO: This indicates that the SNMP scan was attempted, but the probe did not receive any valid responses from the target device using the configured SNMP community strings. This may occur if SNMP is disabled on the target device, the configured community strings in the "Discovery Credentials" do not match those set on the device, or SNMP communication is being blocked by a firewall or network restriction. We recommend verifying that SNMP is enabled on the device, ensuring the correct community strings are configured, and confirming that the probe’s IP is allowed to query SNMP.

MESSAGE: '/usr/local/bin/nmap';' Result: stdOut
SYMPTOM: Linux not converting to probe
INFO: The agent is unable to detect Nmap at the /usr/bin/nmap path. Nmap may be installed in a different directory. In such cases, create a symbolic link from the actual Nmap installation path to /usr/bin/nmap. For example: sudo ln -s /path/to/your/nmap /usr/bin/nmap. To identify the current Nmap installation path, run: which nmap. After creating the symbolic link, unmap and remap the credentials using the Map Discovery & Credentials option on the agent from the portal.

MESSAGE: nats: insufficient resources
SYMPTOM: Repeated sql error coming from Probe agent
INFO: A large number of IP ranges are currently mapped to the agent, resulting in increased workload on the server, which may lead to low-memory conditions. We recommend distributing some of the IP ranges to another dedicated agent and monitoring the system performance afterward.

MESSAGE: C:\ProgramData\Package Cache\
SYMPTOM: Application detections in this directory could cause false-positives.
INFO: This is not a standard installation path. The Package Cache is a hidden Windows installer cache that stores repair and uninstall resources as well as bundled components used by other applications. Even if the main application is removed, executable files may remain, and since the binaries still exist, the agent detects them. To prevent these components from being considered, disable the "Package Cache" option under Global Settings (either per company or globally). Once disabled, the agent will no longer include components located in the Package Cache during software and vulnerability evaluation. After applying this change and allowing the next agent scan to complete, the detections will automatically clear.
